paper_title: TRIAGE: Dialectical Reasoning for Explainable Risk Prediction on Irregularly Sampled Medical Time Series with LLMs

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Clinical early warning systems built on electronic health records, in which clinical observations are recorded as irregularly sampled medical time series (ISMTS), must deliver both calibrated risk scores for patient triage and interpretable rationales that clinicians can verify. Large Language Models (LLMs) have been explored for this task, yet they collapse graded clinical risk into overconfident binary predictions. This risk polarization undermines both calibration and cross-patient comparability. To address this, we propose TRIAGE, a framework that trains an LLM to generate dialectical reasoning over competing clinical outcomes by eliciting outcome-specific rationales. This dialectical formulation mitigates risk polarization, enabling a single LLM to yield continuous risk scores grounded in explicit clinical reasoning. Evaluated on three ISMTS benchmarks, TRIAGE achieves an average AUPRC improvement of 3.3% and reduces calibration error by 81% compared to the competitive baselines. An LLM-as-a-judge assessment further shows that our rationales surpass post-hoc explanations from the baseline by 20% in clinical reasoning quality. The source code is available at https://github.com/HyeongWon-Jang/TRIAGE .

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Predicting adverse events such as mortality or disease onset from Electronic Health Records (EHRs) is central to early-warning systems in critical care. Clinical observations in EHRs, such as vital signs and laboratory tests, are sparsely recorded at irregular intervals with substantial missingness and thus commonly treated as irregularly sampled medical time series (ISMTS) (Sun et al., 2020). Beyond simply predicting the correct outcome, an effective clinical decision support system necessitates two properties that existing systems rarely provide together: well-calibrated, cross-patient comparable *Equal Contribution. 1 Table 1: Comparison of LLM-based approaches for ISMTS risk prediction. Cont.: continuous risk score; NL: natural language reasoning. Methods Example Risk Cont. NL Implicit probability (HeLM, EHR-R1) Reasoning + Answer (KARE, OpenTSLM) Q: Sepsis6h? A: Yes Q: Sepsis6h? A: [Rationale] Yes 0.73 1 TRIAGE Q: Sepsis6h? A: [Rationale] Yes 0.89 risk score for triage and resource allocation, and natural language explanation grounded in reasoning over clinical knowledge that clinicians can evaluate and trust. Existing approaches address these requirements only in isolation. Specialized deep learning models for ISMTS (Che et al., 2018; Horn et al., 2020; Luo et al., 2024) achieve strong predictive performance through dedicated architectures tailored to irregular sampling and inter-variable dependencies but produce opaque predictions with no accompanying rationale. Post-hoc explainability methods, including general-purpose techniques (Lundberg and Lee, 2017; Sundararajan et al., 2017; Shrikumar et al., 2017) and those designed for time series (Crabbé and Van Der Schaar, 2021; Liu et al., 2024; Jang et al., 2025), can identify contributing variables or time points, but their explanations remain non-linguistic and limited to per-point attribution scoresnot the higher-level abstractions that clinicians reason over. To bridge this gap, large language models (LLMs) are natural candidates, as they internalize broad medical knowledge and can articulate complex reasoning in natural language. Yet existing LLM-based approaches for ISMTS reproduce this same divide. As in Table 1, two strategies have emerged, each delivering only one of the two properties. One line of work (e.g., HeLM (Belyaeva et al., 2023) and EHR-R1 (Liao et al., 2025)) extracts continuous risk score from the models implicit answer-token probability, which is known to be better than verbalized probability (Gu et al., 2024), thereby treating the LLM as classifier without any natural language reasoning. The other (e.g., KARE (Jiang et al., 2025) and OpenTSLM (Langer et al., 2025)) elicits reasoning alongside the prediction but optimizes solely for the discrete answer, yielding risk estimates that are not comparable across patients. As first step, we scrutinize this challenge through preliminary study, examining why current LLMs struggle to provide both continuous risk scores and clinically grounded explanations. We uncover risk polarization problem: when natural language reasoning is elicited before risk scoring, the score is pushed toward the extremes. First, rationales typically pre-commit to single outcome (e.g., Therefore, this patient is likely to die. Answer:), making the final prediction nearly deterministic given the preceding biased text. Second, rationales are typically one-sided, presenting only evidence that supports the committed outcome rather than evidence bearing on all possible outcomes. This issue is exacerbated in ISMTS, where trajectories contain coexisting signals of deterioration and stabilization. Consequently, simply prompting an LLM to reason and then predict is fundamentally insufficient for jointly obtaining clinically grounded explanations and cross-patient comparable risk scores. Motivated by these observations, we propose Time Series Reasoning by Inspecting Alternative for Grounded Risk Estimation Outcomes (TRIAGE), framework that addresses risk polarization by grounding risk estimation in dialectical reasoning over alternative outcomes. Rather than steering reasoning toward single outcome, TRIAGE generates dedicated rationale for each candidate outcome and derives the final risk from the LLMs implicit probability conditioned on these rationales, jointly delivering continuous risk estimates and natural language explanations in single pass. TRIAGE fosters this through two-stage training pipeline: (i) Dialectical Reasoning Supervision followed by (ii) Self-Refinement, yielding well-calibrated and discriminative risk estimates. To summarize, our contributions are threefold: We identify risk polarization problem in LLM-based clinical risk prediction on ISMTS: eliciting reasoning collapses the implicit probability because (1) rationales pre-commit to single outcome, driving the probability to near-certainty, and (2) rationales exhibit onesided confirmation bias rather than weighing alternative outcomes. To address this, we propose TRIAGE, which decomposes reasoning into outcomeconditioned rationales and derives risk from the resulting implicit probability distribution, replacing one-sided rationalization with dialectical deliberation over alternative outcomes. Extensive experiments show that TRIAGE, instantiated on small open-source LLM, improves AUPRC by 3.3% and reduces calibration error by 81% over various strong baselines, while producing more clinically reasonable rationales grounded in patient-specific evidence.

sections.title: 2 Related Work
sections.content: Irregularly sampled time series. Prior work has developed specialized architectures for irregularly sampled time series, including RNN-based models (Che et al., 2018), ODE-based continuoustime models (Chen et al., 2018; Rubanova et al., 2019), set-based encoders (Horn et al., 2020), interpolation-based models (Shukla and Marlin, 2019), attention-based models (Shukla and Marlin, 2021; Zhang et al., 2023), transformer-based models (Tipirneni and Reddy, 2022; Li et al., 2023), and graph-based models (Zhang et al., 2022; Luo et al., 2024, 2025). While these methods advance representation learning and predictive accuracy on irregular time series, they do not produce natural language explanations. In contrast, we study LLMbased clinical risk prediction from irregularly sampled medical time series, where risk estimates are coupled with textual reasoning. LLMs for clinical time series. LLM-based methods for clinical time series, spanning both general time series modeling and EHR-based prediction, fall into two dominant categories. The first, answertoken risk scoring, treats the LLM as classifier and extracts the risk from its answer-token probability (Belyaeva et al., 2023; Liao et al., 2025). The second, reasoning with hard-label prediction, produces rationale with discrete class label optimized for correctness (Jiang et al., 2025; Liu et al., 2025a; Langer et al., 2025). separate line of work 2 offloads prediction to neural model and uses the LLM only for auxiliary rationales, contextual summaries, or discrete predictions (Nguyen et al., 2024; Lee et al., 2025; Jiang et al., 2026). We instead keep reasoning and risk scoring within single LLM that generates natural language reasoning and produces patient-comparable risk grounded in it.

sections.title: 3 Problem Setup
sections.content: We formulate clinical risk prediction from ISMTS as supervised classification problem. Let = {(si, zi, yi)}N i=1 denote cohort of patients. The temporal record si is multivariate ISMTS represented as si = {(ti,k, vi,k, mi,k)}Li k=1, where Li is the total number of observations and each tuple specifies the time ti,k, value vi,k, and variable index mi,k {1, . . . , D} of the k-th measurement among clinical variables. The static descriptor zi Rds encodes time-invariant patient attributes such as age and gender, and yi {0, . . . , 1} is the clinical outcome label among classes. Our objective is to learn predictor that outputs patient-specific class distribution pi = (pi,0, . . . , pi,C1), where pi,c 0 is the probability that patient belongs to class c. While discrete prediction can be obtained as ˆyi = arg maxc pi,c, our primary quantity of interest is the probability vector, which yields discriminative risk estimate over the candidate outcomes for each patient.

sections.title: 4 Preliminary Study
sections.content: In this section, we identify the causes of standard LLM reasonings failure at clinical risk prediction. natural approach is to have the LLM reason over the patient record before prediction so that the risk score is grounded in explicit clinical evidence. Through preliminary study, we show that this approach instead collapses the score distribution into degenerate extreme. We term the resulting failure the risk polarization problem and identify its two underlying causes. Setup. We study in-hospital mortality prediction with gpt-oss-120b (Agarwal et al., 2025), reporting both discrimination (AUROC, AUPRC) and calibration (ECE, Brier score (BS)). The risk score refers to the models implicit probability: the softmax probability assigned to the positive-class answer token at the logit level, rather than any verbalized number (motivated in Appendix A.2). Full details are in Appendix A. Reasoning causes risk polarization. We compare reasoning-then-answer, where the model generates rationale before predicting (e.g., [Rationale] Yes/No), against an answer-only baseline (e.g., Yes/No without reasoning). Under the answer-only baseline, the predicted-class probability averages 86.4% 18.8% across patients, providing sufficient spread to differentiate cases. Introducing reasoning completely collapses this signal: the predicted-class probability exceeds 99.98% on every patient with near-zero variance, leaving no basis for cross-patient ranking. Reasoning is typically pre-committed. Under the reasoning-then-answer paradigm, regardless of the preceding reasoning quality, rationales tend to terminate in an explicit verdict immediately before the answer position (e.g., Therefore, this patient is likely to die. Answer:). To quantify this tendency, we use gpt-oss-120b itself as an LLM-as-judge to classify whether each generated rationale closes with such committed verdict, and find that 71.7% of rationales exhibit this pattern (judge prompts are provided in Appendix G). Once such verdict appears in the prefix, it dominates the conditioning context. Although the original clinical evidence remains part of the context, the answer token is overwhelmingly steered by the nearby verdict, concentrating the predicted-class probability on the outcome already stated in the rationale. The rationale thus acts as self-imposed hard prior that absorbs residual uncertainty before the label is produced. Table 2: Results on MIMIC-III in-hospital mortality prediction with gpt-oss-120b. AUROC (%), AUPRC (%), ECE, and BS are computed over 10 sampled responses. Method AUROC AUPRC ECE BS Standard Two-sided 76.1 75.5 27.8 30.2 0.236 0.205 0.211 0. Reasoning is typically one-sided. Furthermore, we uncover that beyond the final verdict, the reasoning itself lacks balanced deliberation. Inspecting individual traces reveals that the model exhibits confirmation bias, committing to one outcome and citing only supportive evidence while disregarding countervailing signals (see Appendix A.4). This conflicts with clinical trajectories, where indicators of deterioration and stabilization may coexist (Jang et al., 2025). To test whether this tendency is systematic, we append minimal two-sided instruction: Weigh the evidence for survival and 3 Figure 1: Overview of TRIAGE. (Upper) TRIAGE represents irregular patient records as language inputs and inspects alternative outcomes to estimate patient risk. (Lower) The training pipeline consists of dialectical reasoning supervision followed by self-refinement. for in-hospital death, then decide. This improves AUPRC and reduces ECE and Brier score with comparable AUROC  (Table 2)  , confirming onesided reasoning as systematic limitation and motivating dialectical deliberation as better inductive bias. In summary, our preliminary study yields two design principles. (P1) Reasoning must be structured to preserve continuous risk score rather than collapse into single committed outcome. (P2) Reasoning should weigh the evidence for each candidate outcome separately.

sections.title: 5 TRIAGE
sections.content: Guided by the design principles from Section 4, in this section, we propose Time Series Reasoning by Inspecting Alternative Outcomes for Grounded Risk Estimation (TRIAGE). TRIAGE introduces two components: (i) reasoning procedure in which outcome inspection addresses P2 by generating dedicated rationale for each candidate outcome, and risk estimation addresses P1 by deriving graded risk from the models implicit outcometoken distribution (Section 5.1); and (ii) two-stage training pipeline of Dialectical Reasoning Supervision followed by Self-Refinement (Section 5.2). Figure 1 provides an overview of the framework, and the detailed algorithm is provided in Appendix B.

sections.title: Outcomes
sections.content: This section details TRIAGEs reasoning procedure, organized into three components: input representation, outcome inspection, and risk estimation. The procedure follows the two principles from Section 4. It inspects each candidate outcome through dedicated rationale (P2) and reads risk from the models implicit outcome-token distribution at fixed answer position (P1). This design preserves continuous risk signal rather than collapsing into single committed outcome. Input representation. Following set-based encoding for irregular time series (Horn et al., 2020), we serialize only the observed measurements in variable-centric format rather than representing the full observation matrix. The input prompt xi for patient concatenates structured task definition P, textualized time-invariant attributes tzi (e.g., age, gender), and temporally ordered observations tsi: xi = concat(P, tzi, tsi). (1) An example prompt is provided in Figure 6. Outcome inspection. As shown in Section 4, standard LLM reasoning tends to align with single outcome direction, citing only supportive evidence while disregarding countervailing signals. This one-sided tendency is especially problematic for ISMTS, where patient trajectories frequently exhibit concurrent indicators of both deterioration and stabilization (Jang et al., 2025). To address this, TRIAGE generates dialectical reasoning chain that separately examines each candidate outcome before producing final prediction (Figure 1(a)). For binary prediction task with 4 candidate outcomes = {y+, y}, the model produces dedicated rationale ryk for each yk Y, articulating the evidence from the patients observations that supports that specific outcome: chain = [ry1, ry2, ˆy], {y1, y2} = {y, y+}, (2) where ryk is the rationale for outcome yk, ˆy is the final predicted outcome token. The order of the two rationales is interchangeable. This structure realizes P2 by requiring the model to consider both sides of the clinical evidence before reaching decision, surfacing signals that one-sided reasoning would discard. Risk estimation. Even with dialectical rationales, reasoning chain that concludes with verdict before the answer token reintroduces the risk polarization identified under P1: rationale commits to one direction, driving the next-token probability to nearcertainty. To preserve graded risk signal, TRIAGE terminates the reasoning chain with ## Final Decision header followed directly by single outcome token, without any intermediate summary or verdict. We map each outcome yk to designated token (e.g., 0 for negative, 1 for positive) and extract the corresponding logit ℓk at this fixed position. The risk score is then computed as: (yk x) = exp(ℓk)/ (cid:80) exp(ℓk). Because the preceding rationales present evidence for both outcomes without committing to conclusion, the models internal distribution at this position reflects graded assessment, yielding continuous and cross-patient comparable risk estimates rather than the near-deterministic outputs observed with standard reasoning.

sections.title: 5.2 Training Pipeline
sections.content: The reasoning procedure described above does not emerge from standard prompting or conventional fine-tuning. Thus, we introduce novel two-stage pipeline of TRIAGE: Stage 1 instills the structured reasoning behavior via supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on synthesized outcome-conditioned rationales, and Stage 2 refines the model through reinforcement learning (RL) on its own samples to improve both discrimination and calibration. Stage 1: Dialectical reasoning supervision. The goal of this stage is to teach the model to produce outcome-conditioned rationales. As illustrated in the left panel of Figure 1(b), we elicit rationales from strong LLM separately for each candidate outcome: the LLM is prompted to assume that given outcome holds and to identify only the patient features that support it. This process must adhere to two constraints. First, the LLM should not reference any alternative outcome, ensuring that each rationale remains strictly outcome-specific rather than contrastive. Second, the LLM should not fabricate unobserved evidence. In cases where no supporting evidence exists, the rationale is left blank. These constraints yield concise, feature-grounded rationales while discouraging hallucinated or contrastive content. Full prompts are provided in Appendix G. The resulting rationales are concatenated with the ground-truth answer token to form reasoning traces following Equation 2. Since the rationale ordering is interchangeable, we include both orderings as data augmentation. small LLM is then fine-tuned on these traces with standard SFT. Stage 2: Self-refinement. After SFT on offpolicy dialectical reasoning traces built from strong model, the model can generate high-quality rationales. However, it does not fully address the traininginference mismatch (Ranzato et al., 2015; Bengio et al., 2015), where the model must condition on its own generated prefixes at inference time rather than on reference trajectories. To address this limitation, we further post-train the model with Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO; Shao et al., 2024). The cross-entropy objective is applied only to the final decision token to supervise the implicit predictive probability, whereas the preceding reasoning tokens are optimized by the GRPO objective: L(θ) = LGRPO(θ) + λ LCE(θ), (3) LGRPO(θ) = 1 Lr (cid:88) (cid:88) j=1 τ j (Jj,τ (θ) βDKL(πθ πref)) , (cid:88) j , Jj,τ (θ) = min Lr = (cid:16) wj,τ (θ) ˆAj,τ , wj,τ (θ) ˆAj,τ (cid:17) , j=1 wj,τ (θ) = clip(wj,τ (θ), 1 ϵlow, 1 + ϵhigh) , where denotes the reasoning-token positions excluding the final decision token, wj,τ (θ) = πθ(rj,τ x,rj,<τ ) (rj,τ x,rj,<τ ) is the token-level importance ratio, πθold and ˆAj,τ is the advantage obtained by normalizing the group-level rewards {Rj}G j=1. sections.title: 6.1 Experimental Setup
sections.content: Datasets and metrics. We conduct experiments on three irregular medical time series benchmarks, P12 (Silva et al., 2012), P19 (Reyna et al., 2020), and MIMIC-III (Johnson et al., 2016). P19 involves predicting sepsis onset within 6 hours, while P12 and MIMIC-III both target in-hospital mortality prediction. For discrimination, we report the area under the ROC curve (AUROC) and the area under the precision-recall curve (AUPRC). Given the severe class imbalance, we treat AUPRC as the primary discrimination metric (Davis and Goadrich, 2006; Saito and Rehmsmeier, 2015). For calibraTable 4: Calibration results on P12, P19, and MIMICIII. ECE and Brier score (BS); lower is better. The best results are indicated in bold. Baselines. We compare against representative ISMTS baselines, namely GRU-D (Che et al., 2018), mTAND (Shukla and Marlin, 2021), SeFT (Horn et al., 2020), Raindrop (Zhang et al., 2022), STraTS (Tipirneni and Reddy, 2022), ViTST (Li et al., 2023), KEDGN (Luo et al., 2024), and Hi-Patch (Luo et al., 2025). We further include GPT-5.11 (OpenAI, 2025) and gpt-oss-120b (Agarwal et al., 2025) as zero-shot LLMs. Implementation details. To collect training data, we use GPT-5.1 (OpenAI, 2025) for P12 and P19, and Kimi K2 Thinking2 (Team et al., 2025) for MIMIC-III. Since the datasets are highly imbal1GPT-5.1 is not evaluated on MIMIC-III, as PhysioNets Credentialed Data Use Agreement prohibits transmitting MIMIC data to third-party LLM APIs (https://physionet. org/news/post/llm-responsible-use/). 2We run Kimi K2 Thinking inference locally for MIMICIII to comply with PhysioNets credentialing requirements. 6 Figure 2: Performance comparison under the leave-variables-out setting on P12 and MIMIC-III. anced, we apply oversampling following prior work (Zhang et al., 2022; Li et al., 2023; Luo et al., 2024), collecting multiple distinct rationales per minority sample through repeated LLM inference rather than duplicating identical samples. We apply two-stage training pipeline to Qwen3-4BBase (Yang et al., 2025).

sections.title: 6.2 Main Results
sections.content: We evaluate TRIAGE on discrimination (AUROC, AUPRC; Table 3), calibration (ECE, Brier score; Table 4), and robustness under missing variables during inference (Figure 2). Predictive performance. As shown in Table 3, zero-shot frontier LLMs (GPT-5.1 and gpt-oss120b) rank at the bottom with average ranks of 10.50 and 11.67. This indicates that generalpurpose reasoning alone does not transfer well to clinical risk prediction on ISMTS. With SFT alone, TRIAGE already reaches an average rank of 4.25, on par with the strongest ISMTS baselines, GRUD (3.42), KEDGN (4.00), and STraTS (4.08). After RL, TRIAGE achieves the best average rank of 1.58, placing first or second on every metric. Against the strongest baseline GRU-D, TRIAGE yields relative improvements of 0.8% in mean AUROC and 3.3% in mean AUPRC. For calibration  (Table 4)  , SFT alone brings TRIAGE to parity with the ISMTS baselines, though it still trails zero-shot GPT-5.1 by small margin. Adding RL reduces the mean ECE by 80% and the mean Brier score by 49%, achieving the best scores on every benchmark. Robustness under limited information. In practice, clinical risk predictors rarely observe every variable at training time due to sensor failures, protocol differences, or missing records. Following Luo et al. (2024), we randomly mask from 10% to 50% of variables in the validation and test sets of Table 5: Ablation on the reasoning structure, on P12 with Qwen3-4B-Base. P12 and MIMIC-III while keeping the training set unchanged. Figure 2 shows that TRIAGE remains robust as variables are removed. On P12, TRIAGE matches the strongest baseline on AUPRC but trails slightly on AUROC. On MIMIC-III, it leads on AUPRC at every masking ratio and on AUROC at four out of five. sections.title: 7 Further Analysis
sections.content: In this section, we ablate the components of TRIAGE (Section 7.1) and examine whether the generated rationales are clinically meaningful (Section 7.2). Additional experiments and analyses are provided in Appendix E.

sections.title: 7.1 Ablation Study
sections.content: 5 structure. Table Reasoning compares TRIAGE against zero-shot inference and two SFT variants. Answer-only SFT removes reasoning entirely and trains the model to predict the label directly, treating the LLM as classifier backbone. One-sided rationale SFT follows the common practice of supervising reasoning toward the ground-truth label only, using the correct-outcome traces from our collected data. Detailed prompts are in Appendix G. Answer-only SFT substantially improves over zero-shot inference but provides no clinical justification. We employ three judge models: GPT-5.1 (OpenAI, 2025), Claude Sonnet 4.5 (Anthropic, 2025), and Gemini 3 Flash (Google, 2025). We query each judge three times per sample and report the mean of the nine resulting scores. As shown in Table 6, TRIAGE achieves higher total IDEA score (7.744 vs. 6.474). The largest gain appears on the interpretive summary criterion (+0.902), indicating that the model captures the patients baseline risk and temporal trajectory more effectively. The alternative diagnosis dimension also shows notable improvement (+0.288), in line with our design choice to reason over alternative outcomes, while the remaining criteria perform comparably. Qualitative results in Appendix further illustrate that TRIAGE produces more patient-specific and clinically grounded reasoning traces.

sections.title: 8 Conclusion
sections.content: We have introduced TRIAGE, framework that grounds clinical risk estimation in reasoning over alternative outcomes rather than single committed trajectory. By inspecting each candidate outcome before deriving the final risk from the LLMs implicit probability, our approach addresses the risk polarization problem that arises when reasoning is elicited before risk scoring. With dialectical reasoning supervision and self-refinement on small open-source LLM, TRIAGE outperforms various competitive baselines while producing rationales that align with how clinicians weigh patient evidence. More broadly, this work signals path forward for LLM-based clinical decision support, where predictive performance and grounded explanation are pursued together rather than as competing objectives. (a) Discrimination. (b) Calibration. Figure 3: Comparison of discrimination and calibration performance based on the reward design on P12 data. probability collapses toward 1.0. Even after averaging predictions over 10 sampled outputs at 10 inference cost, it underperforms Answer-only SFT on both metrics, confirming that one-sided reasoning supervision can actively harm risk estimation. In contrast, TRIAGEs dialectical reasoning achieves the best AUROC (86.9%) and AUPRC (56.4%) while retaining clinically grounded explanations. RL reward design. We compare our batch-level reward against conventional sample-level reward, in which each rollout receives Rsample = log (yi xi, ri,j) followed by the same group normalization. This reward provides direct correctness signal for each patient, akin to the standard outcome-based reward in GRPO, but does not explicitly encourage risk estimates that are comparable across patients. i,j Figure 3 shows that the batch-level reward yields noticeable improvements in both discrimination (Figure 3a), with higher AUPRC, and calibration (Figure 3b), with lower ECE and Brier scores. These results confirm that the inter-sample signal introduced by the batch-level reward provides complementary learning objective that improves both the separability and calibration of predicted risk scores.

sections.title: 7.2 Reasoning Analysis
sections.content: Unlike existing ISMTS models, which require chaining post-hoc XAI method with an LLM interpretation step to produce linguistic explanations, TRIAGE generates clinical rationales directly. To compare the quality of these explanations, we conduct an LLM-as-a-judge evaluation on 200 randomly sampled cases on the P12 dataset. As baseline, we apply Integrated Gradients (IG; Sundararajan et al., 2017) to STraTS (the second-best model on P12) and prompt GPT-5.1 to interpret the explanation. Following the IDEA assessment tool (Baker et al., 2015; Schaye et al., 2021), each judge evaluates reasoning traces along four dimen-

sections.title: Limitations
sections.content: Our work has several limitations. First, our evaluation is restricted to binary prediction tasks, and extension to multi-class or multi-label clinical settings is left for future work. Second, the LLMbased pipeline is also considerably more expensive than lightweight baselines such as GRU-D, both in training and at inference time, since the model generates multi-step rationales before producing prediction. This overhead is acceptable for typical clinical deployment but may be prohibitive where strict low-latency inference is required. Third, for reasoning evaluation, we use LLM-as-a-judge with the IDEA assessment tool (Baker et al., 2015; Schaye et al., 2021) in place of clinical expert assessment. We mitigate model-specific bias by aggregating across diverse judges and grounding our analyses in medical literature, but expert evaluation remains an important next step. Finally, generated rationales may contain inaccuracies or biases. TRIAGE is research prototype, not clinical tool, and its outputs should not substitute for qualified clinical judgment.

sections.title: Ethical Considerations
sections.content: MIMIC-III is accessed under the PhysioNet credentialed Data Use Agreement.3 Accordingly, no stage of our MIMIC-III pipeline relies on thirdparty LLM services: preprocessing, rationale generation, training, and evaluation (including all zeroshot LLM baselines) are performed locally with self-hosted, open-weight models.

sections.title: Implicit Probabilities as Risk Scores
sections.content: To choose an appropriate risk score, we compare two ways of eliciting mortality probabilities from the LLM. The verbalized probability pverb [0, 1] is the numeric estimate in the models response text, while the implicit probability (y=1 prompt) is read off the next-token distribution over the answer tokens. Prior work reports better discrimination from implicit probabilities on binary medical prediction (Gu et al., 2024); we revisit this in our setting. For controlled comparison, this preliminary study is run on MIMIC-III with reasoning disabled (reasoning_effort=None), on gpt-oss-120b and Qwen3-8B (Yang et al., 2025) for the concentration analysis. Discrimination and calibration. Table 7 reports the comparison. The implicit probability improves AUPRC at small cost to AUROC and reduces ECE and Brier score by roughly 4. The cause is anchor concentration in verbalized outputs  (Table 8)  : seven values cover 75.3% of gpt-oss-120b Figure 4: AUPRC of the outcome-based and verbalized risk scores under reasoning, on MIMIC-III with gpt-oss120b. estimates and three values cover 63.5% of Qwen38B estimates. These anchors reflect response priors rather than patient-level risk, flattening ranking and inflating calibration error. The implicit probability varies continuously over [0, 1] and preserves fine-grained differences. Trainability. second reason favors the implicit channel: it admits training signal. The implicit probability is differentiable function of the next-token logits, whereas the verbalized output is produced by discrete sampling and is nondifferentiable with respect to model parameters. For any gradient-based alignment of model risk estimates with patient outcomes, the verbalized output is structurally unusable. Taken together, we adopt the implicit probability as the risk score throughout. A.3 Verbalized Probabilities for Zero-Shot

sections.title: Evaluation
sections.content: For zero-shot LLM benchmarking under reasoning, two candidates are available for graded risk score: (i) an outcome-based estimator that approximates the models underlying probability by averaging binary answers across many sampled responses, and (ii) the verbalized probability stated in single response. Figure 4 compares the two in AUPRC on MIMIC-III with gpt-oss-120b. The outcome-based estimator surpasses the verbalized score only at around 30 sampled responses per question, at proportional inference cost. The verbalized probability is clean single-number readout from single response, providing far more efficient zero-shot signal. We therefore adopt it whenever reporting zero-shot LLM baselines in subsequent sections. 12 Algorithm 1 TRIAGE Inference: Dialectical Reasoning over Alternative Outcomes Input: Policy πθ, patient with prompt template P, timeinvariant features tzi , observations tsi , outcome set = {y, y+} 1: xi concat(P, tzi , tsi ) 2: Sample [ry1 , ry2 , "## Final Decision"] πθ( xi) 3: Read logits ℓ0, ℓ1 at the token position after the header 4: Compute (y+ xi) = exp(ℓ1)(cid:14) (cid:80) Output: Risk estimate (y+ xi) exp(ℓk) A.4 One-Sided Reasoning Traces We observe that LLM reasoning traces typically confirm single outcome rather than weigh evidence on both sides. MIMIC-III is credentialed dataset, so we do not reproduce raw patient records here. We instead draw an illustrative case from the publicly available PhysioNet 2012 Challenge (P12) (Silva et al., 2012), which targets the same task as MIMIC-III (in-hospital mortality prediction from early ICU trajectories) and exhibits the same one-sidedness under our prompting protocol. To make this behavior easy to inspect, we set reasoning_effort=None for gpt-oss-120b and prompt the model with Provide your clinical reasoning, then state your final prediction. This places the entire rationale in the visible response, where the one-sided pattern is straightforward to identify. Figure 7 shows two reasoning traces out of ten sampled from gpt-oss-120b on the same patient. One trace cites only deterioration signals and predicts death; the other cites only stabilization signals and predicts survival. Each trace is internally coherent, yet neither weighs evidence from the opposite direction.

sections.title: B Algorithm
sections.content: Algorithm 1 and Algorithm 2 outline the detailed procedure of TRIAGE used in our experiments.

sections.title: C Experimental Setup Details
sections.content: C.1 Datasets We evaluate our method on three representative medical time series classification benchmarks: P12 (Silva et al., 2012), P19 (Reyna et al., 2020), and MIMIC-III (Johnson et al., 2016). Table 9 summarizes their key statistics. All three are binary classification tasks with severe class imbalance, where the positive class forms small minority in every case. Following prior works (Zhang et al., 2022; Li et al., 2023), we use five random 8:1:1 splits for P12 and P19, since neither dataset provides an official partition. For MIMIC-III, we adopt the fixed 7:1.5:1.5 split defined by Harutyunyan et al. (2019) and run five random seeds. In both settings, we report the mean and standard deviation across the five runs. We provide detailed description of each dataset below, including our preprocessing refinements over the original releases. P12. The P12 dataset (Silva et al., 2012) is taken from the PhysioNet 2012 Challenge.4 Following Zhang et al. (2022); Li et al. (2023), we exclude 12 inappropriate samples, leaving 11,988 ICU patient records. Each record consists of irregularly sampled measurements from 36 sensors collected during the first 48 hours of an ICU stay, together with 5 static demographic features (Age, Gender, Height, ICUType, Weight). The task is binary in-hospital mortality prediction. We adopt the training, validation, and test splits released by Raindrop (Zhang et al., 2022). In addition, we correct parsing misalignment in the original preprocessing, where the five demographic features are extracted by row position rather than by parameter name. When sensor reading at t=00:00 is interleaved with the demographic rows, the two streams contaminate each other. Real sensor values are then parsed as demographics, while later static fields such as Weight are pushed into the time series. This affects 207 of 12,000 patients (1.7%) and produces implausible values such as Height = 1 cm. P19. The P19 dataset (Reyna et al., 2020) is taken from the PhysioNet 2019 Challenge on sepsis prediction.5 Each sample contains 34 irregularly sampled time series variables together with 6 static features (Age, Gender, Unit1, Unit2, HospAdmTime, ICULOS). The task is binary classification of whether sepsis will occur within the next 6 hours. The public release used by prior work (Zhang et al., 2022; Li et al., 2023; Luo et al., 2024) comprises 38,803 ICU stays. In addition, we exclude 65 patients whose 34 time series variables contain no non-zero finite observation within their reported sequence length, yielding 38,738 samples. Notably, all 65 excluded patients carry positive sepsis label. sections.content: #Samples #Variables #Avg. obs. Missing ratio Pos. rate P12 P19 MIMIC-III 11,988 38,738 21,107 36 34 16 401 238 431 84.9% 80.5% 65.5% 14.2% 4.0% 13.2% them reduces the positive count from 1,626 to 1,561, lowering the positive rate from 4.19% to 4.03%. Such records provide no measurement signal for any time series classifier. Retaining them inflates the apparent positive rate without contributing usable information, which is particularly problematic given the already low positive rate of P19. Because we deviate from the sample set used by prior work, we generate new reproducible 5 splits for all experiments on P19. MIMIC-III. MIMIC-III (Johnson et al., 2016) is publicly released critical care database distributed via PhysioNet.6 We adopt the benchmark prepro6https://physionet.org/content/mimiciii/1.4/ cessing pipeline of Harutyunyan et al. (2019) and target binary in-hospital mortality prediction over the first 48 hours after ICU admission. The resulting dataset comprises 21,107 patient stays, each described by 16 time series variables and one static feature (Height). C.2 Metrics AUROC and AUPRC follow their standard definitions. The Brier score is the mean squared error between the predicted probability and the binary outcome. ECE (Guo et al., 2017) measures the average gap between predicted confidence and empirical accuracy across = 10 equal-width bins on [0, 1]. For both Brier score and ECE, lower values 14 indicate better calibration. C.3 Baselines We employ two categories of baselines: zero-shot LLMs and ISMTS baselines. ISMTS baselines, we use For the zero-shot LLMs, we use GPT-5.1 (OpenAI, 2025) with reasoning_effort=medium and gpt-oss-120b with reasoning_effort=high. Based on the preliminary study in Appendix A.3, we use the verbalized probability produced by the model as the predicted risk. The prompt details for the zero-shot evaluation are provided in Appendix G. all For the Adam (Kingma and Ba, 2014) optimizer. To mitigate class imbalance, we follow the training protocol of (Zhang et al., 2022; Li et al., 2023; Luo et al., 2024) and upsample the minority class threefold while constraining each mini-batch to 1:1 class ratio. AUPRC is the primary metric for irregular time series classification under class imbalance (Davis and Goadrich, 2006; Saito and Rehmsmeier, 2015). We therefore use validation AUPRC as our model selection criterion. Unless otherwise specified, we train each model for up to 50 epochs with an early stopping patience of 5. For each hyperparameter configuration, we conduct 5 runs and select the one with the highest mean validation AUPRC. Method-specific hyperparameter configurations and search spaces are described below. GRU-D (Che et al., 2018). We reproduce the original authors Keras implementation7 in PyTorch with the original MLP classification head. Following the released code, we fix the input and recurrent dropout to 0.3. We tune the hidden dimension over {32, 64, 128, 256, 512, 1024}, the learning rate over {5e-4, 1e-3, 5e-3, 1e-2}, and the batch size over {32, 64, 128, 256, 512}. mTAND (Shukla and Marlin, 2021). We follow the official implementation8 and adopt the mTANDFull classification variant. Following the released code, we fix the number of reference time points to 128, the time-embedding dimension to 128, the number of attention heads to 1, and the generator hidden size to 50. We tune the recurrent encoder hidden size over {32, 64, 128}, the latent dimension over {32, 64, 128}, the classification-loss 7https://github.com/PeterChe1990/GRU-D 8https://github.com/reml-lab/mTAN weight over {1, 5, 10, 50, 100}, the learning rate over {5e-4, 1e-3, 5e-3, 1e-2}, and the batch size over {32, 64, 128}. SeFT (Horn et al., 2020). We reproduce the authors TensorFlow/Keras implementation9 in PyTorch. Following the values dominant across datasets in the original papers hyperparameter table, we fix the observation encoder to 4-layer MLP, the attention aggregator to 2 layers with 4 heads, and the output readout to 2-layer MLP of width 512. We tune the per-observation embedding dimension over {256, 512}, the observation encoder MLP width over {64, 128}, the shared dropout rate over {0.0, 0.2, 0.4}, the timeencoding dimension over {8, 16}, the maximum time scale over {100, 1000}, the learning rate over {1e-4, 5e-4, 1e-3, 5e-3}, and the batch size over {32, 64, 128}. Raindrop (Zhang et al., 2022). We follow the official implementation10. Following the original paper, we fix the batch size to 128. We tune the per-sensor observation embedding dimension over {2, 4, 8}, the number of Raindrop layers over {1, 2, 4}, the number of attention heads over {1, 2, 4}, dropout over {0.0, 0.2, 0.4}, and the learning rate over {1e-4, 5e-4, 1e-3, 5e-2}. STraTS (Tipirneni and Reddy, 2022). We follow the official PyTorch implementation11. Following the released code, we fix the learning rate to 5e-4, the batch size to 32, and the per-patient observation cap to 880. We tune the hidden dimension over {32, 64, 128}, the number of Transformer layers over {1, 2, 4}, the number of attention heads over {8, 16, 32}, and the shared dropout rate (used for both feature and attention dropout) over {0.0, 0.2, 0.4}. ViTST (Li et al., 2023). We follow the official implementation12 and adopt the Vision-Text variant, which combines Swin Transformer image encoder with RoBERTa text encoder, fine-tuning both end-to-end. sections.title: Implementation Details
sections.content: Data collection. We collect outcome-specific rationales using GPT-5.1 for P12 and P19, and Kimi K2 Thinking (Team et al., 2025) for MIMIC-III. We first collect rationale separately for each candidate outcome. For each candidate, the LLM is prompted to assume that outcome and to identify only the patient features that support it. To keep each rationale strictly outcome-specific rather than contrastive, we prohibit the LLM from referencing any alternative outcome or fabricating unobserved evidence; when no supporting evidence appears, the rationale is left blank. These constraints suppress hallucinated and contrastive content, yielding concise, feature-grounded rationales. To mitigate class imbalance, we follow prior work (Zhang et al., 2022; Li et al., 2023; Luo et al., 2024) and oversample the minority class during data collection. Specifically, for P12 and MIMIC13https://github.com/easonLuo2001/KEDGN 14https://github.com/easonLuo2001/Hi-Patch Table 10: SFT Experimental setup details. Parameter Value batch_size max_length 64 (P12) 128 (P19/MIMIC-III) 12,288 (P12/MIMIC-III) 11,264 (P19) 3 num_train_epochs AdamW optimizer 2 105 learning rate cosine lr_scheduler_type 0.05 warmup_ratio 0.01 weight_decay max_grad_norm 1.0 completion_only_loss true III, we collect three rationales per candidate outcome, while for P19, which has lower positive ratio, we collect six rationales per candidate outcome. The full set of prompts is provided in Appendix G. SFT. Following prior work (Zhang et al., 2022; Li et al., 2023; Luo et al., 2024), we employ custom sampler that maintains 1:1 minority-tomajority ratio within each batch. All SFT experiments are conducted on 1 H200 GPU. The full set of hyperparameters is listed in Table 10. RL. For RL initialization, we select the best SFT checkpoint before 2 epochs rather than the best checkpoint over all three SFT epochs, as this empirically yields better RL performance and aligns with recent observations that high SFT scores may not predict better post-RL performance (Kang et al., 2025). Following Dr.GRPO (Liu et al., 2025c) and DAPO (Yu et al., 2026), we compute advantages without standard-deviation normalization and use token-level policy gradient loss. For KL regularization, we adopt the k2-as-loss formulation (Liu et al., 2025b). To address class imbalance, the advantage is scaled by factor of 2 for minority class samples and by 2 for majority class samples, where : 1 denotes the ratio of the majority to minority class size in the full training set. Particularly, in the case of the P19 data, the minority class sample is oversampled by factor of three due to severe class imbalance. We also employ custom stratified sampler to ensure that each rollout batch, rather than the PPO mini-batch, matches the class ratio of the full training set. All RL experiments are implemented on top of the verl (Sheng et al., 2024) and performed on 2 B200 GPUs. The full set of training hyperparameters is in Table 11. We use = 1.0 as the margin 16 Table 11: RL Experimental setup details. Note that the batch size is set to 256, which is larger than typical configurations, in order to ensure that our batch-level reward operates stably. Inference. We use fixed sampling temperature of 0.7 during inference. Because the model is trained on both rationale orderings in Equation 2 to improve generalization, we likewise perform inference under each. For each pass, we apply softmax to the logits of y+ and at the ˆy position and take the resulting probability of y+ as the risk score. The patients final risk score is defined as the mean of these two probabilities. comparison between this bidirectional averaging and using each ordering individually is provided in Appendix E.3. Leave-Variable-Out Full Results We report the full numerical results for the leavevariable-out experiments. Table 12 and Table 13 list the AUPRC and AUROC for every method at masking ratios of 10%, 20%, 30%, 40%, and 50% on P12 and MIMIC-III, respectively.

sections.title: E Additional Experiments and Analyses
sections.content: E.1 Low-Resource Training Experiments Clinical applications frequently suffer from limited labeled data, particularly for rare conditions and newly emerging diseases. These regimes favor models that can effectively leverage pre-trained medical and reasoning priors. Unlike ISMTS baselines trained from scratch, TRIAGE builds on pre-trained LLM and inherits such priors directly, without requiring extensive task-specific supervision. Setup. To probe this advantage under data scarcity, we fine-tune TRIAGE with rsLoRA (Kalajdzievski, 2023), which preserves pre-trained representations more reliably than full fine-tuning in low-data regimes. We use rank = 64, scaling factor α = 64, and dropout 0.05, applying adapters to all attention and MLP projection layers (q_proj, k_proj, v_proj, o_proj, gate_proj, up_proj, down_proj). We omit the RL stage to isolate the data efficiency of supervised reasoning alone. We subsample only the training set of P12 at fractions of 1%, 5%, and 10%, keeping the validation and test sets intact for consistent evaluation. Baseline tuning. For fair comparison, we start from the hyperparameters of the full-data setting and additionally sweep smaller batch sizes and broader range of learning rates. This ensures that observed differences reflect data efficiency rather than under-tuned baselines. Results. Figure 5 reports the results. TRIAGEs improvement over baselines is most pronounced in the lowest-data regime. At P12-1%, our method outperforms the strongest baseline GRU-D by 4.4% AUROC and 11.1% AUPRC. The margin diminishes as labeled data becomes more abundant, and the two methods perform comparably at P12-10%. This pattern is consistent with the hypothesis that pre-trained knowledge contributes most when supervision is scarce. Full per-fraction results are provided in Table 15. Figure 5: Performance under low-resource training. We subsample P12 at 1%, 5%, and 10% training fractions and compare TRIAGE against ISMTS baselines using AUROC and AUPRC. E.2 Ablation Study on the Backbone To verify robustness to backbone choice, we apply TRIAGE to Qwen3-1.7B and 8B (Yang et al., 2025) in addition to our 4B default, and further to Llama 3.2 3B (Grattafiori et al., 2024) across architecture families. As reported in Table 14, TRIAGE 17 retains consistent advantage over the corresponding baselines on every backbone, suggesting that our reasoning supervision generalizes beyond single model. E.

sections.title: Inference Direction Analysis
sections.content: Since TRIAGE is trained on both rationale orderings, we examine whether averaging the risk scores from the two directions at inference offers any benefit over using single ordering. Table 16 reports the results on P12 with Qwen3-4B-Base under the SFT setting. The three strategies perform comparably, indicating that single-direction pass alone already yields reliable risk estimate and can serve as lower-cost alternative. Nonetheless, bidirectional averaging consistently provides small additional gain (e.g., AUPRC of 56.4 vs. 56.1/56.2), and we adopt it as the default inference strategy.

sections.title: F Qualitative Results
sections.content: To qualitatively assess the clinical reasoning of our model beyond the quantitative evaluation based on IDEA (Baker et al., 2015; Schaye et al., 2021), we conduct manual analysis of the generated reasoning paths. Table 17 and Table 18 present representative case studies from the P12 dataset, demonstrating how the model integrates clinical indicators to infer patient outcomes. In Table 17, we examine patient classified as survival and compare our models reasoning with that of the STraTS baseline, whose decision basis is analyzed through post-hoc XAI attribution followed by LLM interpretation. The STraTS attributions contain several signals that are inconsistent with established medical knowledge. For example, Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) score of 15, which indicates fully intact consciousness (Munakomi et al., 2026), is attributed as evidence for mortality. Similarly, bicarbonate (HCO3) level below 22 mEq/L is used to support survival prediction, despite such values generally being considered low and potentially indicative of metabolic acidosis (Castro et al., 2024). In contrast, our models reasoning paths exhibit no such contradictions. The two approaches also diverge in their treatment of temporal dynamics. For urine output, STraTS relies on cross-sectional assessment of near-normal values observed at later time steps, whereas our model captures the trajectory from initially low output through progressive recovery over time, which serves as clinically meaningful indicator of improving renal perfusion (Khwaja, 2012). This temporal perspective enables more comprehensive and grounded clinical reasoning. Furthermore, we show another patient classified as mortality in Table 18. The STraTS attributions again contradict established clinical knowledge: white blood cell (WBC) count of 23.3, substantially exceeding the normal range and suggestive of leukocytosis (Mank et al., 2026), is attributed as evidence supporting survival. Despite Blood urea nitrogen (BUN) being well-established prognostic marker in critically ill populations (Arihan et al., 2018), BUN levels of 98-99 are also used to support survival prediction, despite being severely elevated (Hosten, 1990). Furthermore, the most clinically critical indicators in this case, potassium level of 10.0 mEq/L, representing severe hyperkalemia with the risk of fatal cardiac arrhythmia (Simon et al., 2017), and elevated Troponin T, well-recognized marker of myocardial injury 15, do not appear among the major contributors in the STraTS attribution. In contrast, our model explicitly incorporates these values into its reasoning chain. In addition, when identifying potential evidence for survival in case dominated by mortalityassociated signals, STraTS produces attributions that lack clinical plausibility, whereas our model identifies clinically reasonable counterevidence, such as temporal recovery trends in serum potassium. These observations suggest that our approach yields more balanced reasoning. It is important to acknowledge, however, that certain limitations remain. Specifically, in the patient case of Table 17, body temperature exhibited complex, non-monotonic trajectory characterized by an initial hypothermic risk, partial recovery, and subsequent decline. Neither the baseline nor our models generated reasoning fully captures this intricate behavior. This indicates that reasoning over variables with highly fluctuating temporal trajectories remains challenge. Furthermore, to assess hallucinations in our LLMs outputs, we employ an LLM-as-a-judge to detect severe hallucinations in our reasoning traces, such as references to features or values that were not present in the patient record (see Figure 24 for the full prompt). Out of 200 samples from the P12 dataset, we identified severe hallucinations in only 3 cases (1.5%). Evaluation: Prompt for IDEA assessment tool (2/4) ## Interpretive Prognostic Summary, 0-4 points This assesses whether the reasoning gives an accurate, patient-specific, temporally aware summary of the patients main prognostic problem. Prefer reasoning that accurately identifies, when supported by the patient features: 1. Key baseline or contextual mortality-risk factors: - Examples: age, major comorbidities, chronic organ disease, frailty-relevant context, admission context, or other baseline risks. 2. Main acute ICU problem or organ dysfunction: - Examples: respiratory failure, shock or hemodynamic instability, renal dysfunction, metabolic acidosis, neurologic depression, hepatic or coagulation dysfunction, infection signal, multi-organ dysfunction, or other acute problems explicitly supported by the data. 3. Illness time course or trajectory: - Correctly distinguishes worsening, improving, fluctuating, or stable course. - Gives special attention to persistent instability or late deterioration near the end of the observation window. 4. Clinically meaningful prognostic abstractions: - Examples: persistent hypotension, escalating oxygen needs, worsening renal function, severe acidosis, multiorgan dysfunction, improving hemodynamics, stable oxygenation, transient isolated abnormality. - These abstractions must be grounded in the provided data. Scoring anchors: - 4: Accurate, patient-specific, and temporally aware summary that captures the main acute problem, key relevant baseline/contextual risks if provided, trajectory, and meaningful prognostic abstractions. - 3: Mostly accurate summary with the main prognostic problem identified, but with minor omission, limited temporal interpretation, or limited abstraction. - 2: Partially accurate summary; mentions some relevant patient-specific factors but misses an important main problem, trajectory, or severity distinction, or includes minor unsupported statements. - 1: Minimal or generic summary; only one relevant patient-specific element is accurately identified, or the reasoning mostly lists data without integrating the main prognostic problem. - 0: No meaningful prognostic summary, or the summary is mostly contradicted by the patient features or dominated by hallucinated information. Do not penalize the reasoning for not mentioning information that is not provided. Do penalize it for inventing missing baseline risks, diagnoses, treatments, or organ failures. ## Differential Prognostic Assessment, 0-2 points This assesses whether the reasoning considers competing prognostic interpretations and explicitly prioritizes the most plausible mortality-risk interpretation. For this ICU mortality task, differential means competing mortality-risk hypotheses, not diagnostic differential diagnosis. Relevant competing interpretations include: - High vs moderate vs low mortality risk. - Mortality risk driven by multi-organ dysfunction vs lower risk because abnormalities are mild or transient. - High-risk deteriorating trajectory vs stabilization or survival trajectory. - Risk-increasing signals vs risk-decreasing signals. - Uncertainty due to mixed or incomplete evidence. Scoring anchors: - 2: Clearly states lead mortality-risk interpretation and explicitly discusses at least one plausible alternative or countervailing interpretation, then prioritizes the lead interpretation using patient-specific data. - 1: Gives lead mortality-risk interpretation but alternatives are implicit, generic, weakly discussed, or not clearly prioritized; or lists competing ideas without clearly weighing them. - 0: No meaningful prognostic assessment; only bare conclusion, generic statement, or diagnostic laundry list that does not support mortality-risk reasoning. If the evidence is strongly one-directional, the reasoning can still receive 2 points if it explicitly explains why alternative interpretations are less supported and acknowledges remaining uncertainty appropriately. Figure 21: Prompt used for the quantitative evaluation of generated reasoning trace (2/4). We adopt IDEA assessment tool as the evaluation criteria. 35 Evaluation: Prompt for IDEA assessment tool (3/4) ## Explanation of Lead Prognostic Judgment, 0-2 points This assesses whether the reasoning explains why its lead mortality-risk judgment follows from the objective patient data. Prefer reasoning that: - Clearly links objective patient-specific evidence to the lead mortality-risk judgment. - Uses clinically relevant data such as vital signs, laboratory values, organ-support indicators, severity scores, comorbidities, or temporal trends when provided. - Interprets the severity and persistence of abnormalities appropriately. - Gives final risk interpretation that is proportional to the evidence. Calibration requirements: - Very high mortality risk requires strong evidence, such as severe or persistent instability, major organ dysfunction, multiple converging high-risk features, or marked late deterioration. - Very low mortality risk should not ignore major organ dysfunction, severe derangements, or sustained deterioration. - Mild, isolated, or transient abnormalities should not be overstated as extreme risk. - If evidence is mixed, the reasoning should express appropriate uncertainty. Scoring anchors: - 2: Uses at least two objective patient-specific data points or temporal trends to support the lead judgment, interprets them clinically and accurately, and gives calibrated final mortality-risk interpretation. - 1: Provides some patient-specific support, but the explanation is incomplete, weakly linked, based on only one main data point, partially miscalibrated, or contains minor unsupported claims. - 0: No explanation of the lead mortality-risk judgment, or the explanation is unsupported, contradicted by the data, dominated by hallucinated facts, or purely generic. Do not give full credit for simply naming abnormal values without explaining their prognostic relevance. ## Alternative Prognostic Explanation / Counterevidence, 0-2 points This assesses whether the reasoning identifies and explains patient-specific evidence that could support different, lower-risk, higher-risk, or more uncertain interpretation. For this ICU mortality task, alternative diagnosis explained is adapted to mean alternative prognostic explanation or counterevidence. Relevant counterevidence may include: - Reassuring or risk-decreasing factors. - Stable or improving organ function. - Transient rather than sustained abnormalities. - Lack of support for severe conditions that the reasoning might otherwise imply. - Risk-increasing evidence that contradicts an overly reassuring conclusion. - Mixed evidence requiring uncertainty. - Absence of provided evidence for major interventions or complications, without treating missing data as definitely normal. Scoring anchors: - 2: Identifies and explains at least two patient-specific countervailing data points, alternative prognostic explanations, or sources of uncertainty, and weighs them fairly against the lead judgment. - 1: Mentions one patient-specific countervailing data point or gives generic/limited discussion of uncertainty or alternative risk interpretation. - 0: No meaningful counterevidence or alternative prognostic explanation; cherry-picks only evidence supporting the conclusion; or treats missing information as definitely normal or definitely abnormal. Counterevidence can point in either direction: - If the lead judgment is high mortality risk, counterevidence may include stabilization, improvement, preserved organ function, or transient abnormalities. - If the lead judgment is low mortality risk, counterevidence may include persistent instability, organ dysfunction, severe derangements, or late deterioration. Mostly accurate and clinically grounded, with minor omissions, limited counterevidence, or mild calibration issues. - 5-6: Mixed reasoning. Some accurate patient-specific evidence, but important omissions, weak temporal reasoning, one-sided assessment, or moderate calibration problems. - 3-4: Weak reasoning. Generic, poorly grounded, several unsupported claims, major omissions, or significant misreading of the clinical picture. - 0-2: Poor reasoning. Prompt used to detect severe hallucination in reasoning traces You are clinical evidence-grounding judge for ICU mortality/survival rationales. You will be given: 1. Observed patient information from the first 48 hours of ICU stay. 2. rationale explaining why the patient is predicted to die or survive. Your task is to judge whether the rationale is grounded in the observed patient information, or whether it contains serious hallucination: concrete clinical facts that are completely absent from the patient data. Return True if the rationales substantive clinical evidence is present in, paraphrased from, summarized from, or reasonably inferred from the patient information. Return False if the rationale uses at least one clinically meaningful fabricated fact as evidence, such as diagnosis, treatment, event, demographic detail, lab/vital value, trend, medication, procedure, or condition that is not present in the patient information or is clearly contradicted by it. Be lenient. The goal is to detect serious hallucination, not minor imprecision. Do not mark False for small wording issues, approximate values, rounding, broad ranges, mild exaggeration, or imperfect temporal alignment. If value or feature appears anywhere in the patient data and the rationale refers to it in broadly consistent way, consider it grounded. For example, if the patient data contains PaO2 values of 98, 100, and 125 mmHg, then rationale saying PaO2 around 98125 mmHg indicating adequate oxygenation should be considered grounded, even if the exact timing is not perfectly aligned with another feature such as mechanical ventilation. Do not require every cited feature to co-occur at the exact same timestamp unless the rationale makes very specific timing claim that is central and clearly contradicted. Accept clinical interpretations such as stable, adequate oxygenation, renal dysfunction, shock, or high mortality risk if they are reasonably supported by the observed measurements. Do not evaluate whether the mortality/survival prediction is medically correct; only judge whether the rationale is based on information actually observed in the input. Mark False only when there is clear, material unsupported claim, for example: - The rationale cites lactate, creatinine, vasopressors, mechanical ventilation, sepsis, pneumonia, cancer, renal failure, surgery, or another condition/treatment/event that is not present anywhere in the patient data. - The rationale gives concrete numeric value or range that is grossly inconsistent with the observed values. - The rationale describes trend that is clearly opposite to the observed trend. - The rationale relies on medical history or outcome information not included in the patient data. If the unsupported statement is only minor non-evidentiary phrase and the main clinical evidence is grounded, return True. When uncertain whether something is minor overstatement or serious hallucination, prefer True.
